title: Using be in the present simple
rule: Use am with I, is with he, she and it, and are with you, we and they.
explanation: The verb be changes with the subject.
incorrect_example: She are happy.
corrected_example: She is happy.
error_reason: Use is with she, not are.
keywords: be, am, is, are, present simple

title: Using subject pronouns correctly
rule: Use subject pronouns before the verb: I, you, he, she, it, we, they.
explanation: Subject pronouns replace names and come before the verb.
incorrect_example: Me work in a bank.
corrected_example: I work in a bank.
error_reason: Me is not a subject pronoun.
keywords: subject pronouns, I, you, he, she, it, we, they

title: Using a, an and the
rule: Use a before consonant sounds, an before vowel sounds, and the for a specific noun.
explanation: Articles tell us if a noun is general or specific.
incorrect_example: He bought the book for the first time.
corrected_example: He bought a book for the first time.
error_reason: Use a when the noun is not specific.
keywords: articles, a, an, the

title: Regular plural nouns
rule: Most regular nouns make the plural with -s or -es.
explanation: Add -s to most nouns, and -es after endings like -s, -sh, -ch, -x and -o.
incorrect_example: I saw two dog.
corrected_example: I saw two dogs.
error_reason: Plural nouns usually need -s or -es.
keywords: plurals, singular, plural, -s, -es

title: Present simple for routines
rule: Use the present simple for routines, habits and facts.
explanation: We use it for things that happen regularly or are always true.
incorrect_example: I am getting up at seven every day.
corrected_example: I get up at seven every day.
error_reason: A regular routine needs the present simple, not present continuous.
keywords: present simple, routines, habits, facts

title: Third person singular in the present simple
rule: In the present simple, add -s or -es to the verb with he, she or it.
explanation: The verb changes in the third person singular.
incorrect_example: He play football.
corrected_example: He plays football.
error_reason: Use plays with he.
keywords: present simple, third person singular, -s, -es

title: Present simple negatives with do not and does not
rule: Use do not/does not + base verb in negatives.
explanation: Do not and does not make the sentence negative.
correct_example_2: She does not work on Sundays.
incorrect_example: She does not works on Sundays.
corrected_example: She does not work on Sundays.
error_reason: After does not, use the base form of the verb.
keywords: present simple, negative, do not, does not, don't, doesn't

title: Present simple questions with do and does
rule: Use do or does at the start of present simple questions, then the base verb.
explanation: Do and does help make questions in the present simple.
incorrect_example: Does he speaks English?
corrected_example: Does he speak English?
error_reason: After does, use the base verb.
keywords: present simple, questions, do, does

title: Possessive adjectives before nouns
rule: Use my, your, his, her, its, our and their before a noun.
explanation: Possessive adjectives show who something belongs to.
incorrect_example: This is mine phone.
corrected_example: This is my phone.
error_reason: Mine is not used before a noun.
keywords: possessive adjectives, my, your, his, her, its, our, their

title: Some and any with nouns
rule: Use some in affirmative sentences and any in negatives and questions with countable plural and uncountable nouns.
explanation: Some is common in positive sentences. Any is common in questions and negatives.
incorrect_example: I don't have some money.
corrected_example: I don't have any money.
error_reason: Any is usually used in negatives.
keywords: some, any, countable, uncountable, nouns

title: There is and there are
rule: Use there is with singular nouns and there are with plural nouns.
explanation: Match the verb to the noun that follows.
correct_example_1: There is a shop near here.
correct_example_2: There are two chairs in the room.
incorrect_example: There is two chairs in the room.
corrected_example: There are two chairs in the room.
error_reason: Use are with plural nouns.
keywords: there is, there are, singular, plural

title: This, that, these and those
rule: Use this/these for things near you and that/those for things further away.
explanation: This and these are for near. That and those are for far.
incorrect_example: Those is my keys.
corrected_example: Those are my keys.
error_reason: Use are with those.
keywords: this, that, these, those, demonstratives

title: Much, many and a lot of
rule: Use many with countable plural nouns, much with uncountable nouns, and a lot of in positive sentences with both.
explanation: Choose the quantifier by the type of noun.
correct_example_1: How many books do you have?
incorrect_example: How much books do you have?
corrected_example: How many books do you have?
error_reason: Books are countable, so use many.
keywords: much, many, a lot of, countable, uncountable

title: Present continuous for now
rule: Use am/is/are + verb-ing for actions happening now or around now.
explanation: This tense describes temporary actions and events in progress.
correct_example_1: She is studying at the moment.
correct_example_2: I am talking to a customer now.
incorrect_example: She studies at the moment.
corrected_example: She is studying at the moment.
error_reason: An action happening now needs the present continuous.
keywords: present continuous, now, at the moment, -ing

title: Present continuous for future arrangements
rule: Use the present continuous for fixed future arrangements.
explanation: We often use it for plans already decided.
correct_example_1: I am meeting my friend tomorrow.
correct_example_2: They are flying to Dublin on Friday.
incorrect_example: I meet my friend tomorrow.
corrected_example: I am meeting my friend tomorrow.
error_reason: A planned arrangement usually needs the present continuous.
keywords: present continuous, future arrangements, plans

title: Regular past simple verbs
rule: Regular verbs usually add -ed in the past simple.
explanation: Use the past simple for finished actions in the past.
correct_example_1: We watched a film last night.
incorrect_example: We watch a film last night.
corrected_example: We watched a film last night.
error_reason: A finished past action needs the past simple.
keywords: past simple, regular verbs, -ed, yesterday, last night

title: Irregular past simple verbs
rule: Some common verbs have irregular past forms and do not add -ed.
explanation: Learn common irregular forms such as went, had and saw.
correct_example_1: She went to Italy last year.
correct_example_2: I saw your message this morning.
incorrect_example: She goed to Italy last year.
corrected_example: She went to Italy last year.
error_reason: Go has an irregular past form: went.
keywords: past simple, irregular verbs, went, had, saw

title: Past simple negatives with did not
rule: Use did not + base verb in the negative past simple.
explanation: After did not, the verb stays in the base form.
correct_example_1: I did not see the film.
correct_example_2: They did not arrive on time.
incorrect_example: I did not saw the film.
corrected_example: I did not see the film.
error_reason: After did not, use see, not saw.
keywords: past simple, negative, did not, didn't

title: Going to for plans and evidence
rule: Use be going to for future plans and for things that look certain now.
explanation: It is common for planned actions or clear evidence.
correct_example_1: We are going to visit my aunt next week.
correct_example_2: Look at the clouds. It is going to rain.
incorrect_example: We going to visit my aunt next week.
corrected_example: We are going to visit my aunt next week.
error_reason: Going to needs am/is/are before it.
keywords: going to, future, plans, prediction

title: Comparatives with -er and more
rule: Use -er with short adjectives and more with longer adjectives.
explanation: Use than when comparing two things.
correct_example_1: This bag is lighter than that one.
correct_example_2: Today is more important than yesterday.
incorrect_example: This bag is more light than that one.
corrected_example: This bag is lighter than that one.
error_reason: Light is a short adjective, so use lighter.
keywords: comparatives, -er, more, than

title: Irregular comparative forms
rule: Some common adjectives have irregular comparative forms, such as good/better and bad/worse.
explanation: These forms do not follow the normal rules.
correct_example_1: This solution is better than the first one.
incorrect_example: This solution is gooder than the first one.
corrected_example: This solution is better than the first one.
error_reason: Good has an irregular comparative: better.
keywords: better, worse, irregular comparatives, good, bad

title: Can and cannot for ability and permission
rule: Use can and cannot with the base verb to talk about ability and permission.
explanation: Can does not change with the subject.
incorrect_example: She can to swim.
corrected_example: She can swim.
error_reason: After can, use the base verb without to.
keywords: can, cannot, ability, permission

title: Should for advice
rule: Use should + base verb to give advice or say what is a good idea.
explanation: Should is common when giving recommendations.
incorrect_example: You should to rest more.
corrected_example: You should rest more.
error_reason: After should, use the base verb without to.
keywords: should, advice, recommendation

title: Present perfect for life experience
rule: Use have/has + past participle to talk about experience at an unspecified time before now.
explanation: Do not use a finished past time expression with this use.
incorrect_example: I have been to Paris last year.
corrected_example: I went to Paris last year.
error_reason: Last year is a finished past time, so use the past simple.
keywords: present perfect, experience, ever, never, have seen

title: Present perfect with since and for
rule: Use since with a starting point and for with a period of time.
explanation: Both often describe situations that started in the past and continue now.
correct_example_2: She has worked here for six months.
incorrect_example: We have lived here for 2018.
corrected_example: We have lived here since 2018.
error_reason: Use since with a point in time.
keywords: present perfect, since, for, duration

title: Already and yet with the present perfect
rule: Use already in affirmative sentences and yet in questions and negatives.
explanation: Already means before now. Yet means up to now, often in questions or negatives.
correct_example_1: I have already finished my homework.
incorrect_example: I have yet finished my homework.
corrected_example: I have already finished my homework.
error_reason: Yet is not used like already in an affirmative sentence.
keywords: present perfect, already, yet

title: First conditional for real future possibilities
rule: Use if + present simple, will + base verb for possible future results.
explanation: It describes a real or possible situation in the future.
correct_example_1: If it rains, we will stay at home.
correct_example_2: If you study, you will pass the test.
incorrect_example: If it will rain, we will stay at home.
corrected_example: If it rains, we will stay at home.
error_reason: In the if-clause, use the present simple, not will.
keywords: first conditional, if, will, future possibilities

title: Second conditional for unreal situations
rule: Use if + past simple, would + base verb to talk about unreal or imaginary situations.
explanation: It is common for hypothetical present or future situations.
correct_example_1: If I had more time, I would travel more.
correct_example_2: If she lived nearer, she would visit us often.
incorrect_example: If I would have more time, I would travel more.
corrected_example: If I had more time, I would travel more.
error_reason: In the if-clause, use the past simple, not would.
keywords: second conditional, if, would, imaginary situations

title: Passive voice with be + past participle
rule: Form the passive with be + past participle. The object becomes the subject.
explanation: Use the passive when the action is more important than who does it.
incorrect_example: The letter sent yesterday.
corrected_example: The letter was sent yesterday.
error_reason: The passive needs a form of be and a past participle.
keywords: passive voice, be, past participle, was sent, is spoken

title: Using by in passive sentences
rule: Use by to say who or what does the action in a passive sentence.
explanation: By introduces the agent.
correct_example_1: The house was built by my grandfather.
correct_example_2: The song was written by a famous singer.
incorrect_example: The house was built from my grandfather.
corrected_example: The house was built by my grandfather.
error_reason: Use by, not from, to show the agent in the passive.
keywords: passive, by, agent

title: Gerunds after certain verbs
rule: Some verbs are followed by a gerund, for example enjoy, finish and mind.
explanation: After these verbs, use the -ing form.
incorrect_example: I enjoy to read in bed.
corrected_example: I enjoy reading in bed.
error_reason: Enjoy is followed by a gerund, not an infinitive.
keywords: gerund, -ing, enjoy, finish, mind

title: Infinitives after certain verbs
rule: Some verbs are followed by an infinitive with to, for example want, hope and decide.
explanation: After these verbs, use to + base verb.
incorrect_example: I want start early.
corrected_example: I want to start early.
error_reason: Want is followed by to + verb.
keywords: infinitive, to + verb, want, hope, decide
